The event that paved the way for David’s reign happened around the time he was greatly troubled. Those who entrust their concerns to the Lord will calmly accept his will. It demonstrates that he did not desire Saul's death, and he was not eager to ascend the throne.

David mourned sincerely for Saul; and everyone with him humbled themselves under the hand of God, which lay heavily upon Israel because of this defeat. The man who brought the news, David had put to death, as a murderer of his prince. David did not act unjustly in this matter; the Amalekite confessed the crime. If he did what he said, he deserved to die for treason; and his lying to David, if indeed it was a lie, proved, as sin eventually proves, that he was lying to himself. By this action, David showed himself zealous for public justice, without considering his own advantage.

Kasheth, or “the bow,” likely was the title of this mournful funeral song. David does not praise Saul for what he was not; and says nothing about his piety or goodness. Jonathan was a dutiful son, Saul an affectionate father, therefore they were dear to each other. David had reason to say that Jonathan’s love for him was extraordinary. Next to the love between Christ and his people, that affection which arises from it produces the strongest friendship. The troubles of the Lord's people, and the triumphs of his enemies, will always grieve true believers, whatever benefits they may gain from them.
